title-group.article-title: Modeling of conductivity for carbon black nanocomposites incorporating network concentration, interphase conductivity and tunneling dimensions

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/02f71a260grid.510490.9Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering Research Group, Department of Interdisciplinary Technologies, Breast Cancer Research Center, Motamed Cancer Institute, ACECR, Tehran, Iran
2https://ror.org/02gqgne03grid.472279.d0000 0004 0418 1945College of Engineering and Technology, American University of the Middle East, 54200 Egaila, Kuwait
3https://ror.org/01zqcg218grid.289247.20000 0001 2171 7818Department of Mechanical Engineering College of Engineering , Kyung Hee University , Yongin, Republic of Korea

The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Despite the numerous experimented works on the conductivity of carbon black (CB) polymer nanocomposites (PCBs), the modeling methods remain imperfect and require further investigation. Herein, a simple and applicable model for estimating the PCB electrical conductivity is proposed by measurable and meaningful features of CB nanoparticles, interphase, network and tunnelling zone among nearby CBs. Our model also incorporates the significant terms such as the percolation onset, polymer – CB interfacial tension, the percentage of CB and interphase contributing to the network and interphase conductivity (σi). Many experimented conductivities of real PCBs and parametric checkups are used to verify the suggested model. The thickest interphase (t = 20 nm) with the highest conductivity (σi = 400 S/m) yields the conductivity of 7 S/m, while the composite is insulated by the thinnest interphase with the poorest conductivity (t = 3 nm and σi < 250 S/m). Also, the slimmest tunnels (λ = 1 nm) and their poorest polymer resistivity (p = 30 Ω.m) raise the conductivity to 2.2 S/m. However, bigger tunnels (λ > 5.5 nm) highly weaken the conductivity to 0.1 S/m. Accordingly, the characteristics of interphase and tunnels largely handle the conductivity of PCBs.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Carbon black (CB) has immense surface area, high thermal/electrical conductivity, and desirable stiffness1–7. CB nanoparticles are an ideal nanofiller for the reinforcement of polymer nanocomposites, since they can improve the stiffness and conductivity of composites, simultaneously. The small CBs can produce a low percolation onset in the composites and thus only a low amount of CB particles can create a conductive network transferring the electrons (Fig. 1). Chen et al.8 stated that PS/PP (60 wt%/40 wt%) composites with CB nanoparticles (particle size of 20–40 nm) shows the electrical percolation threshold by 1.5 wt% CB. Also, the conductivity of system raised with the addition of CB concentration and reached 0.1 S/m at 3.5 wt% of CB. Also, it was reported that CB nanoparticles with the particle size of 30–35 nm yielded the percolation onset of 5 wt% in the polycarbonate nanocomposites and provided the conductivity of 1 S/m at 10 wt% CB. These data indicate that a low volume of CB nanoparticles can provide a desirable conductivity in the nanocomposites facilitating their applications for sensors, electronics, actuators and energy storage9–11.

An interphase zone is commonly created in polymer nanocomposites, since the connections of the polymer chains with the nanofiller are too strong and nanoparticles have immense surface area12–17. Actually, the interphase contains the polymer chains around the nanoparticles, which has the dissimilar chemical/physical properties from both the polymer and particles (Fig. 1). The reinforcing role of interphase in nanocomposites has been investigated in many articles18–21. Besides, the interphase can be connected to create the network in nanocomposites, which significantly improve the stiffness and conductivity22,23. The interphase was reported in the PCBs24,25; but its impact on the formation of networks and the conductivity of PCBs was not investigated in the earlier reports.

caption.p: Depiction of dispersed and networked CB nanoparticles, interphase and tunneling area.

sec.p: The main mechanism controlling the electrical conductivity in nanocomposites is electron tunneling through adjacent nanoparticles26–32. The electron tunneling was mentioned in the PCBs, since the elections can be moved via the nearby CB nanoparticles (Fig. 1)25,33. Consequently, the characteristics of tunneling region like tunneling resistance and distance mainly handle the electron transfer in nanocomposites34,35. However, the past studies on the conductivity of PCBs rarely studied the tunneling features and their role in the charge transportation.

Many literature reports have studied the electrical conductivity of PCBs by measuring the conductivity at various CB concentrations, but the modeling studies are restricted and imperfect, which limit their optimization. Earlier papers have utilized the power-law model to describe the conductivity in the PCs using the volume fraction of CB and percolation threshold36–38. While the power-law model aligns well with the experimented data, it cannot account for the interphase and tunneling significances on the charge transferring of PCBs. Accordingly, developing more accurate and applicable models is crucial for optimizing the material design and reducing the costs for the fabrication of performant PCBs.

In the current work, we develop a simple, complete and applicable model for the conductivity of PCBs by the expressive, measurable and meaningful features of CB nanoparticles, interphase and their networks as well as tunnelling zone among nearby CBs. Although some reports have addressed the tunneling characteristics and charge transfer, our model includes the significant terms such as the percolation threshold, polymer – CB interfacial tension, the percentage of CB and interphase contributing to the network, tunneling dimensions and the interphase conductivity by a simple and meaningful methodology simultaneously. We used experimental data from various samples, along with the parametric evaluations to support the precision and readability of the developed model. This model is a simple and novel framework for forecasting the conductivity in the PCBs facilitating their optimizing by the meaningful and measurable factors.

sec.title: Model advancement
sec.p: revealing a nonlinear link between PCB conductivity and the concentration of CB/interphase net. Also, both A and B have S/m dimension and associate to the interphase and tunneling features as well as interfacial tension. Notably, both A and B directly manage the nanocomposite conductivity according to Eq. 1. Since the conductivity of insulated polymer matrices is too low as 10− 14−10− 15 S/m, the conductivity of the polymer matrix can be neglected in the developed model. Now, methodical analyses and comparison to experimented conductivity results are used to extract the formulations for A and B.

which makes the common assumption that the interphase has a locally uniform conductivity. But some more sophisticated models account for the fact that the conductivity within the interphase usually varies with radial distance45. Lutz et al.46 reviewed two conceptual models for interphase zones around the spherical fillers in composites: a thin-shell layer within which the local conductivity of the interphase is uniform, and a graded interphase within which the local conductivity varies with radius according to a power law.
which has S/m dimension assuming d (nm), λ (nm) and p (Ω.m). B explicitly includes the tunnelling characteristics correlating the nanocomposite conductivity to tunnelling features.
including the simple and meaningful terms for tunnels and interphase zone.
Also, ϕN as the volume percentage of CB/interphase network in the samples is defined by the measurable features of both CBs and interphase.
where f is the proportion of networked CBs after percolation onset and ϕeff shows the actual concentrations of both CBs and interphase.
where ϕf denotes the CB volume portion, t represents the interphase depth and R is CB radius.
consisting of measurable and meaningful factors of CBs and interphase.
Now, both ϕeff and ϕp can be obtained by simple factors allowing the calculation of f by Eq. 8.

including the determinate and well-defined factors controlling the network concentration in PCBs. ϕN can be substituted into Eq. 6 to estimate the PCB conductivity by an applicable and reasonable methodology.

sec.title: Confirmation of proposed model by measured conductivity
sec.p: Herein, the proposed model (Eq. 6) is validated by the experimented results of conductivity from various PCB examples. The measured data were extracted from the valid articles, since the developed model requires many conductivity results of various PCBs to be certified. Really, the experimented data for one type of examples are not enough to verify a developed model. Consequently, many experimented data from the valid papers were selected to confirm the model. Table 1 tabulates the properties of these examples including CBs (dissimilar size) and poly(vinyl acetate) (PVAc)39, poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF)40, high-density polyethylene (HDPE)41 and polystyrene (PS)42 media, as derived from the references and the proposed model. The percolation threshold (ϕp) of samples measured from the conductivity results were mentioned in Table 1. Also, the calculations of ϕp by Eq. 10 were shown in Table 1, depicting a strong agreement with the measurements from the original references. Thus, Eq. 10 is an applicable and correct expression for ϕp in the PCBs, estimating the interphase size (t) for the samples. t ranges from 0.83 nm to 33 nm. These findings are consistent with the nanoscale results indicating their validity. As the experimented determination of t is a challenging task, the estimation of t by this modeling is appreciated. The various ranges of interphase depth reflect the differences in the interfacial contacts of the polymer medium with the CB particles. Now, the proposed model (Eq. 6) is utilized to estimate the PCB conductivity by the surface energy, density, and electrical conductivity of CB as 55 mN/m50–52, 1.8 g/cm339,53, and 104 S/m54–56, in that order.

caption.p: PCB examples and their characteristics extracted from references and the proposed equations.

sec.p: Figure 2 depicts the predictions of proposed model alongside the measured data of conductivity for the PCB examples. The close alignment of the measured results with the theoretical data disclose the validity of the proposed model predicting the PCB conductivity. This consistent trend demonstrates that the model is valid and applicable for determining the PCB conductivity. The difference among the experimented and estimated conductivities of samples is smaller than 5%, which is acceptable. The significant enhancing of PCB conductivity starts at the percolation onset and thus the variation of conductivity at region above the percolation threshold is noticeable. Actually, a desirable fitness to the experimental data is attained supporting the proposed equation in the current study. This validation enables the developed model to predict the interphase and tunneling characteristics. The experimented conductivity of samples is not zero at very low CB amounts, but the conductivity is too low smaller than 1 S/m, which cannot be shown in Fig. 2. So, it appears to be zero till the critical CB volume fraction named as percolation onset57. In fact, a sharp increment in the PCB conductivity occurs after the percolation onset, which is depicted in Fig. 2. Additionally, the developed model captures this behavior, as it shows a higher conductivity at higher CB amount. Also, the proposed model reveals the significant conductivity of samples after the percolation onset, which agrees with the experimental data.

The σi as interphase conductivity varies from 110 S/m to 700 S/m exhibiting their difference in various samples. The tunneling distance (λ) is attained from 1 nm to 8 nm. Similarly, the contact width (d) ranges from 20 nm to 55 nm for the samples. These values align with the expected nanoscale, further validating the model’s accuracy. Also, p as polymer tunnel resistivity changes from 60 Ω.m to 210 Ω.m. These parameters reflect the poorest tunneling resistance in PVAc/CB sample, since it shows the narrowest tunnels, highest contact diameter and the poorest polymer resistivity. On the other hands, PS/CB composite has the highest tunneling resistance, as it contains the biggest tunnels, smallest contact diameter and the highest p. PVAc/CB and PS/CB samples demonstrate the highest and poorest conductivity among the stated specimens, respectively according to the data in Fig. 2. These findings reveal that the measured conductivity results agree with the tunneling characteristics, as the highest tunnel resistance weakens the nanocomposite conductivity. This consistent trend confirms the validity and reliability of the developed model for the conductivity of PCBs.

caption.p: Tested and estimated results of conductivity by the current model for: (a) PVAc/CB39, (b) PVDF/CB40, (c) HDPE/CB41 and (d) PS/CB42 specimens.

sec.title: Assessment of parameters
sec.p: The effects of all terms on the PCB conductivity (called as conductivity) are evaluated to assess the proposed model (Eq. 6). The average levels of parameters are assumed in all calculations as: R = 15 nm (CB radius), t = 10 nm (interphase size), λ = 4 nm (tunneling size), d = 20 nm (contact diameter), ϕf = 0.05 (CB volume fraction), σi = 180 S/m (interphase conductivity), p = 100 Ω.m (polymer tunnel resistivity), γp = 35 mN/m (polymer surface energy), and γf = 55 mN/m (CB surface tension).

Figure 3 explains the variation in PCB conductivity by the interphase size (t) and conductivity (σi). At t = 20 nm and σi = 400 S/m, the conductivity reaches a peak of 7 S/m. In contrast, the conductivity drops to zero when t = 3 nm and σi < 250 S/m. These results reveal the critical significance of a denser and more-conductive interphase in enhancing the conductivity. Actually, slimmer interphase with less conductivity negatively control the charge transfer. The results disclose that these factors have the most effects on the charge transfer of PCBs.

caption.p: Impressions of interphase size and conductivity on the conductivity: (a) 3-D picture and (b) contour pattern.

sec.p: A denser interphase reduces the threshold of percolation by shortening the spacing among CB particles. Actually, the bigger interphase enlarges the networks, thereby developing the charge transfer within the system23. Contrariwise, a thinner interphase negatively manipulates the percolation onset, since it cannot shorten the distance among CB particles. So, a narrower interphase reduces the effective CB amount (Eq. 9), and restricts the network size, weakening the charge transfer. Thus, the recommended model produces a logical relationship among conductivity and interphase size.

Interphase conductivity has a big role in the overall conductivity of the network, as the interphase is a constituent opf network. Higher interphase conductivity raises the charge transfer via the network, significantly improving the PCB performance. Nevertheless, smaller interphase conductivity limits the network charge transferring and increases its resistance, diminishing the electron mobility and weakens the overall conductivity. Actually, poor interphase conductivity worsens the network’s capacity for effective electron transfer, validating the outputs of the proposed model.

The link among conductivity and CB size and concentration is depicted in Fig. 4. A peak conductivity of 6 S/m is seen at R = 10 nm and ϕf = 0.1, while the conductivity is zero for R > 17 nm or ϕf < 0.05. Accordingly, smaller size of CB particles with higher concentrations are advantageous for enhancing conductivity. However, larger CBs or their lower amounts cannot create the conductivity in the samples. Actually, the conductivity raises by the higher volume of smaller CB nanoparticles.

A lower ϕp and a higher effective filler portion are obtained by smaller CB particles based on Eqs. 9 and 10, which boosts the network amount described in Eq. 8. Actually, smaller CBs grow the interphase concentration, thereby extending the network. Hence, smaller CBs enlarge the network improving the electron transfer. Contrariwise, bigger CB particles raise the ϕp and lessen the interphase amount, shortening the network. Thus, bigger CBs cannot raise the conductivity, which support the important impact of CB radius on the conductivity and certify the model’s accuracy.

caption.p: (a) Three-D imaging and (b) contour plotting of conductivity by the size and volume portion of CB nanoparticles.

sec.p: A higher amount of CBs produces a larger network, intensifying the electron transfer in the samples. Actually, higher volume of CBs strengthens the conductivity part in the PCBs improving the conductivity. Nevertheless, a lower CB amount cannot reach the percolation onset and cause a dispersed phase in the matrix, which prevents the formation of effective conductive pathways, and limits the electron transport and conductivity. Consequently, CB amount directly manages the conductivity, corroborating the modeling results. There are many experimental approaches supporting the direct relation between conductivity and CB amount38,58,59.

Figure 5 reflects the roles of tunneling size and polymer tunnel resistivity in the predicted conductivity. The supreme conductivity of 2.2 S/m is reached by the slimmest tunnels (λ = 1 nm) and poorest resistivity (p = 30 Ω.m). On the other hand, the conductivity mainly weakens to 0.1 S/m when λ > 5.5 nm. Narrower tunnels with poorer tunneling resistance improve the conductivity, but larger tunnels are enough to reduce it. Really, smaller tunnels with poorer tunneling resistance are essential to provide the electron transfer.

caption.p: Dependence of conductivity on the tunneling size and polymer tunnel resistivity by (a) three-D pattern and (b) contour image.

sec.p: Small tunnels have a narrower polymer sheet, which strengthens the electron transfer among adjacent nanoparticles60. Actually, the decreased resistance of smaller tunnels raises the charge transfer, thereby increasing the conductivity. However, larger tunnels contain an insulated polymer film among CBs introducing a high resistance, restricting the electron transfer. Thus, large tunnels create the high resistance to charge transfer, reducing the conductivity. This description reveals that the proposed model precisely relates the conductivity to tunneling distance.

Higher polymer tunnel resistivity (ρ) surges the tunneling resistance12,14, largely limiting the electron tunneling. Elevated ρ weakens the charge flow over the tunnels, dropping the conductivity. Nevertheless, lower ρ falls the tunneling resistance, growing the electron transfer within the system. A lower ρ effectively provides a greater electron tunneling, thereby raising the conductivity. Hence, the developed model correctly relates the conductivity to ρ.

Figure 6 exemplifies the correlation of conductivity with the surface tensions of the polymer (γp) and CB (γf). The peak conductivity of 1.6 S/m occurs at γp = 20 mN/m and γf = 60 mN/m, while γp = 35 mN/m and γf = 40 mN/m produce an insulated sample. A higher polymer surface tension combined with a lower CB surface tension improve the conductivity. contrariwise, higher polymer surface tension with poorer CB surface tension weakens the conductivity. Notably, the highest and lowest surface tensions of polymer and CB, respectively cannot provide the conductivity in the samples.

caption.p: Significance of γp and γf on the PCB conductivity by (a) 3-D image and (b) 2-D pattern.

sec.p: A smaller γp falls the percolation threshold according to Eq. 10, which enhances the network portion (f in Eq. 8) expanding the network and facilitating the electron transport. However, an increase in the γp grows the percolation threshold, which facilitates the dispersion of CB particles in the system. Accordingly, a higher γp produces the smaller networks, weakening the conductivity by reducing the size of conducting network. These findings confirm the estimations of the developed model.

Higher γf increases the interfacial tension among CB nanoparticles and the polymer medium based on Eq. 3, due to bigger differences in surface tensions. The high interfacial tension reduces the CB wettability, elevating the aggregation/agglomeration of CB particles, producing the conductive pathways. Equation 10 reveals that the agglomeration of CBs grows the percolation onset, as the agglomerates have the bigger radius and higher interfacial tension. Conversely, the agglomerated CBs have the smaller distances and big contacts facilitating the charge transfer29. Actually, the agglomerated CBs produce the conductive pathways for charge transfer. However, a smaller γf weakens the interfacial tension, growing the CB dispersion but worsening the aggregation/agglomeration, restricting the electron transfer. Consequently, lower γf negatively influences the conductivity, supporting the developed model.

Figure 7 produces the conductivity predictions by various ranges of contact diameter and network portion. A top conductivity of 0.83 S/m is found at d = 40 and f = 1, whereas the conductivity declines to 0 at f < 0.2. A bigger contact diameter with higher network share positively influence the conductivity. contrariwise, smaller contact diameter and lower network portion weaken the conductivity and the smallest network portion cannot provide the conductivity and produces an insulated sample.

A greater contact diameter widens the contacts among CB nanoparticles which lessens the tunneling resistance, growing the conductivity. Actually, bigger contact diameters create more pathways for electron transfer raising the conductivity34. However, less contact diameter limits the conductive contacts among CBS which increases the tunneling resistance and weakens the conductivity. Really, the contact diameter determines the tunneling resistance which mainly controls the electron tunneling. Accordingly, the conductivity directly links to the contact diameter, validating the recommended model.

caption.p: Stimuli of contact diameter and network portion on the conductivity by (a) 3-D picture and (b) contour scheme.

sec.p: A greater network percentage reflects a larger number of CB nanoparticles within the network, producing a denser and more-conductive network which grows the conductivity. Actually, f directly mentions the size and expanse of network which significantly manages the charge transfer. Conversely, a smaller f reflects a smaller network and higher extent of CB particles dispersed in the samples, limiting the conductivity. As the dispersed CB particles and minute network cannot efficiently transfer the electros, a smaller f produces the less conductivity. So, the proposed model appropriately indicates the critical role of f in the conductivity.

Figure 8 links the conductivity to percolation onset and interfacial tension. The smallest ϕp = 0.01 and the highest γpf = 5 mN/m grow the conductivity to 0.38 S/m, but the conductivity worsens to 0.05 S/m at ϕp = 0.08 and the highest γpf = 1 mN/m. Less percolation onset and higher interfacial tension yield a more conductivity, whereas higher percolation onset with less interfacial tension weaken the conductivity. Actually, providing a less percolation onset with increasing the interfacial tension are necessary to gain a higher charge transfer in the PCBs.

caption.p: (a) Three-D and (b) contour depicting of conductivity by the percolation onset and interfacial tension.

sec.p: Based on Eq. 8, the percolation threshold (ϕp) handless the proportion of CB nanoparticles within the conductive net. A lower ϕp causes more CBs to create the network, facilitating the charge transfer. However, a bigger ϕp provides a greater dispersion of CBs within the system diminishing the network percentage. A bigger ϕp shortens the network which diminishes the charge transfer and conductivity. Hence, higher percolation threshold harmfully affects the charge transfer, supporting the novel model.

The significance of interfacial tension on the conductivity is explained by the quality of CB dispersion or networking within the PCBs. A higher interfacial tension limits the wetting of nanoparticles by the polymer chains, which strengthens their contacts and facilitate the networking. In this status, conductive pathways among contacted CBs yield more conductivity. Contrariwise, less interfacial tension eases the wetting of CBs by the polymer chains, restringing their contacts and providing more dispersed nanoparticles. Really, the smaller interfacial tension shortens the contacts and networking of CBs weakening the conductivity. Consequently, less interfacial tension is advantageous for charge transferring, as stated by the proposed model.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: A simple model was proposed correlating the PCB conductivity to the characteristics of CBs, interphase, network and tunnelling zone. The experimented conductivities of many PCB examples depicted a good harmony with the predictions. Furthermore, the impacts of all factors on the PCB conductivity were plotted and described to confirm the model. t = 20 nm and σi = 400 S/m optimize the conductivity to 7 S/m, but the sample is insulated at t = 3 nm and σi < 250 S/m. Also, a peak conductivity of 6 S/m was attained at R = 10 nm and ϕf = 0.1. However, R > 17 nm or ϕf < 0.05 cannot improve the conductivity. The slimmest tunnels (λ = 1 nm) with the poorest resistivity (p = 30 Ω.m) yield the supreme conductivity of 2.2 S/m, but the conductivity mainly weakens to 0.1 S/m at λ > 5.5 nm. γp = 20 mN/m and γf = 60 mN/m gain the peak conductivity of 1.6 S/m, but γp = 35 mN/m and γf = 40 mN/m produce an insulated sample. Both contact diameter and network portion directly handle the conductivity and a top conductivity of 0.83 S/m is reported at d = 40 and f = 1. Furthermore, the smallest ϕp = 0.01 and the highest γpf = 5 mN/m grow the conductivity to 0.38 S/m, while the conductivity worsens to 0.05 S/m at the highest ϕp = 0.08 and the lowest γpf = 1 mN/m. Overall, denser interphase with more conductivity, smaller size of CB with higher concentration, slimmer tunnels with less resistivity, higher γp with a lower γf, more contact diameter, more network percentage, less percolation onset and higher interfacial tension yield a more charge transfer in the samples. Among the mentioned factors, interphase size and conductivity mainly change the PCB conductivity from 0 to 7 S/m. Also, CB size and amount can vary the nanocomposite conductivity from 0 to 6 S/m. These data indicate the most influence of interphase features and CB size and amount on the charge transfer. The developed model is applicable for only spherical conductive fillers, because the dimension of spherical nanoparticles has been considered in the percolation onset, effective filler amount and network concentration of samples. For development of this model to other fillers, it is crucial to revise the equations for percolation onset, effective filler amount and network concentration.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: Y.Z. and N.G. wrote the main manuscript text. J-H.C. and K.Y.R. revised the paper.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from corresponding authors.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
